title: The Invisible Life of Addie LaRue vs Circe: Head to Head Comparison
metaDescription: Compare The Invisible Life of Addie LaRue vs Circe. V.E. Schwab vs Madeline Miller - which immortal female protagonist's story captivates you more?

book1.title: The Invisible Life of Addie LaRue
book1.author: V.E. Schwab
book1.description: In 1714 France, Addie LaRue makes a Faustian bargain for immortality but is cursed to be forgotten by everyone she meets. For 300 years, she lives an invisible life, leaving no mark on the world—until she meets Henry, a young man who remembers her. Schwab weaves together past and present as Addie and Henry's relationship unfolds against the backdrop of her centuries-long existence. This genre-bending novel explores what it means to live, to be remembered, and to make your mark on the world.

book2.title: Circe
book2.author: Madeline Miller
book2.description: Circe, daughter of the sun god Helios, discovers her gift for witchcraft and is banished to the island of Aiaia. Over centuries, she hones her craft, encounters famous heroes like Odysseus, and struggles to find her place between gods and mortals. Miller transforms Circe from a one-dimensional witch in Homer's Odyssey into a fully realized character exploring themes of power, isolation, motherhood, and self-determination. This feminist retelling gives voice to a character previously defined by men.

book1Strengths: The Invisible Life of Addie LaRue explores immortality curse where nobody remembers you beautifully
V.E. Schwab creates hauntingly romantic premise spanning 300 years of loneliness and fleeting connections
At 448 pages the dual timeline structure between 1714 and 2014 reveals Addie's story gradually
The Faustian bargain with darkness creates compelling supernatural romance beyond typical fantasy
Schwab's lyrical prose elevates the bittersweet exploration of memory, art, and what makes life meaningful
Henry's ability to finally remember Addie after centuries creates emotionally devastating payoff
book1Weaknesses: The slow pacing across 300 years can feel repetitive as Addie faces same curse repeatedly
Some readers find the middle section drags before Henry's introduction changes everything
The ending divides readers between those loving bittersweet conclusion and wanting happier resolution
Contemporary 2014 sections feel less interesting than historical 1714 France periods
book2Strengths: Circe reimagines Greek mythology witch from Odyssey as fully realized sympathetic protagonist brilliantly
Madeline Miller's gorgeous prose brings ancient world alive with sensory rich descriptions throughout
At 385 pages this covers Circe's entire immortal life from childhood to transformation beautifully
The feminist retelling explores witchcraft as power women claim rather than curse or evil
Encounters with gods and heroes like Odysseus feel fresh from Circe's outsider perspective
The isolation theme resonates as Circe finds strength in solitude on Aiaia island
book2Weaknesses: Readers unfamiliar with Greek mythology may miss references and character connections
The episodic structure following immortal life can feel disconnected between major events
Some find the pacing uneven as centuries pass quickly then linger on specific encounters
The ending wraps up Circe's arc but may disappoint readers wanting more adventure

book1: The devil is in the details. That's where you'll find the meaning of life.
You don't need to be remembered to have lived.
A life worth having is a life worth recording.
Time is cruel. And immortality is crueler still.
The beautiful things are worth waiting for.
book2: But in a solitary life, there are rare moments when another soul dips near yours, as stars once a year brush the earth.
When I was born, the word for what I was did not exist.
It is a common saying that women are delicate creatures. If I had ever believed it, I no longer did.
I thought: I cannot bear this world a moment longer. Then, child, make another.
Not every god need be the same.
Humbling women seems to me a chief pastime of poets.

book1: You want a unique immortality curse concept
You want something melancholic and beautiful
You want more recent fantasy fiction
book2: You want feminist Greek mythology retellings
You want encounters with famous heroes
You're interested in witchcraft and magic
You want the more popular choice

verdict.summary: Both books explore immortal women finding agency through centuries but Circe wins with stronger execution and broader appeal. With 4.3 stars and 725,000 reviews versus Addie's 4.1 and 485,000, Circe's feminist mythology retelling resonated more powerfully. Madeline Miller's prose also surpasses Schwab's lyricism. Both deliver beautiful literary fantasy but Circe proves more emotionally satisfying.
verdict.recommendation: Read Circe for gorgeous feminist mythology retelling that reimagines the witch from Odyssey as complex protagonist. At 385 pages with stunning prose it justifies the 4.3 star rating and massive popularity. Miller makes Greek myths feel fresh and deeply human. Read Addie LaRue if you specifically want 300 year romance about immortal cursed to be forgotten. At 448 pages it's longer but rewards with bittersweet meditation on memory and art. Both work but Circe delivers more complete emotional journey with mythology bonus.
